title: Unit Final: Online Identity & Digital Footprints

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains online identity?

choices.text: It is only the username a person chooses online.
choices.feedback: A username is only one part of online identity.

choices.text: It is the password used to enter websites and apps.
choices.feedback: A password protects an account, but it is not an online identity.

choices.text: It is how a person is represented and perceived through their online actions, profiles, and interactions.
choices.feedback: Correct. Online identity is shaped by what a person shares, does, and how others perceive them online.

choices.text: It is only the profile picture attached to an account.
choices.feedback: A profile picture may contribute to identity, but it does not define it by itself.

questions.prompt: Information posted online can influence how other people see and judge a person.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Online content often affects how others view a person.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Online posts, comments, and profiles can shape reputation and perception.

questions.prompt: Which example is the best example of personal information that should usually stay private?

choices.text: A favorite school subject
choices.feedback: This is generally low-risk information.

choices.text: A favorite movie
choices.feedback: This is generally not sensitive personal information.

choices.text: A favorite hobby
choices.feedback: This is usually safe to share in appropriate settings.

choices.text: A home address
choices.feedback: Correct. A home address is sensitive personal information and should usually remain private.

questions.prompt: A student wants to post a picture showing their school ID badge. What is the safest choice?

choices.text: Post it because school IDs are public information.
choices.feedback: School IDs may contain identifying information and should not be casually shared.

choices.text: Avoid posting it because it may expose private information.
choices.feedback: Correct. Avoiding the post protects personal information and reduces risk.

choices.text: Post it only if friends promise not to share it.
choices.feedback: Once posted, control over sharing can be lost.

choices.text: Post it after adding a funny caption.
choices.feedback: A caption does not remove the privacy risk.

questions.prompt: A digital footprint can include posts, comments, photos, likes, and search activity.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. A digital footprint can be made up of many kinds of online activity.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Digital footprints include much more than just posts.

questions.prompt: Why can digital footprints have long-term consequences?

choices.text: Because deleted posts are always erased instantly from everywhere
choices.feedback: Deleted posts may still exist in screenshots, archives, or reposts.

choices.text: Because only adults are affected by online reputations
choices.feedback: Online reputation can affect people of all ages.

choices.text: Because online content can be permanent, copied, and found later
choices.feedback: Correct. Digital content can remain searchable and resurface over time.

choices.text: Because online actions never influence real-world opportunities
choices.feedback: Online behavior can affect trust, relationships, and opportunities.

questions.prompt: A student finds an old embarrassing post on their profile. What is the most responsible action?

choices.text: Leave it because old posts do not matter
choices.feedback: Old posts can still affect reputation and how others view someone.

choices.text: Review the post and remove or update it if it does not reflect the image they want to present
choices.feedback: Correct. Managing older content is part of building a positive digital presence.

choices.text: Share it again to make fun of it before others do
choices.feedback: This may increase attention to the negative content.

choices.text: Ignore it and assume no one can find it
choices.feedback: Old content can often still be found.

questions.prompt: Which action best supports smart posting and profile management?

choices.text: Checking privacy settings and thinking about how a post may affect reputation before sharing
choices.feedback: Correct. Smart posting includes managing privacy and considering consequences.

choices.text: Posting quickly without reviewing the content first
choices.feedback: Smart posting involves pausing and reviewing before sharing.

choices.text: Sharing every detail of daily life to appear more interesting
choices.feedback: Oversharing can create privacy and safety risks.

choices.text: Using any profile picture, even if it is inappropriate or misleading
choices.feedback: Profiles should reflect responsible choices.

questions.prompt: Even if a post is intended for friends only, it can still be copied or shared beyond the original audience.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Posts can be screenshot, copied, or reshared by others.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Private settings reduce risk, but they do not guarantee complete control.

questions.prompt: A student is updating a profile for a school-related online platform. Which choice is most appropriate?

choices.text: Use a fake name and misleading details to seem more interesting
choices.feedback: Misleading profile information can harm trust and credibility.

choices.text: Post private personal details so others know more about you
choices.feedback: Private details should be protected.

choices.text: Choose profile content without thinking about audience or purpose
choices.feedback: Profile management should be thoughtful and appropriate to context.

choices.text: Use a respectful photo and accurate information that supports a positive reputation
choices.feedback: Correct. Appropriate profile choices help build a positive and responsible online presence.
